incorrects.description: Student placed correct factors (6 and 5) but wrong product on first attempt

steps.text: You have the factors right: 6 groups of 5. Now skip-count by 5s six times to find the product.

incorrects.description: Student placed correct product (30) but wrong factors on first attempt

steps.text: The product is right, but check the factors. The problem says 6 groups of 5.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect equation on first attempt (neither factors nor product correct)

steps.text: Not quite. The problem says 6 groups of 5. Start with those factors.

incorrects.description: Student placed correct factors (6 and 5) but wrong product on second attempt

steps.text: Your factors are correct: 6 times 5. Now look at the 5s pattern you learned. Skip-count: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. What comes next?

incorrects.description: Student placed correct product (30) but wrong factors on second attempt

steps.text: You found the right product: 30. But the factors need to match the problem. Read it again: 6 groups of 5. Which number is the groups? Which is the size of each group?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect equation on second attempt (neither factors nor product correct)

params.description: Text hint '6 groups of 5' appears above equation builder.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. 6 groups of 5 means the first factor is 6, the second is 5. Then skip-count by 5s six times: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing tiles: 6 in first slot, 5 in second slot, 30 in product slot. Each placement pauses briefly.

steps.text: Let me show you. 6 groups of 5. First factor: 6. Second factor: 5. Now I skip-count by 5s, six times: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. The product is 30. So the complete equation is 6 times 5 equals 30, which follows the 5s pattern because it ends in 0.
